title: Open Workshop: Paint Disputes, Client Trust, PM Communication, Growth, and Designer Deadlines
summary: This open workshop session featured Erin Stetzer fielding a wide range of real-world challenges from participants including paint quality disputes with clients, broken client trust over a final invoice, project manager communication boundaries around change orders, saying no to work outside your niche, managing designer and architect decision deadlines, and whether to pursue a GC dream or pivot to a project management subcontracting model. Erin provided practical frameworks for resolving disputes with facts over emotion, controlling the narrative on project timelines, and aligning team communication before client-facing conversations. Participants also shared peer insights on growth strategy, scaling carefully, and creative business structures.

teachings.concept: Establish a Builder Standard Before Client Walkthrough
teachings.explanation: Before walking a project with a homeowner for punch-out or quality review, the builder should first walk the space themselves and establish their own standard of approval. This gives the builder authority and clarity, and signals to the homeowner that the builder has done their job. Handing over the blue tape before declaring your own standard undermines the builder's expertise and opens the door to unreasonable complaints.
teachings.quote: We go into that room and we say this is the standard with which we can commit to okay and then we walk that room with the homeowner... I say here's the blue tape or you tell me you tell me what you see.

teachings.concept: Know the Delta Between Contractor Tiers
teachings.explanation: When clients choose a lower-cost trade option, builders must be crystal clear in their own minds about exactly what the quality difference is between tiers. This allows them to defend the work that is within the lower tier's competency, identify what genuinely falls outside of it, and have an honest conversation with the client when issues arise.
teachings.quote: I would be really clear in my head what olympic painter is and what the one in the truck is.

teachings.concept: Separate Facts from Emotion When Resolving Disputes
teachings.explanation: When client disputes arise, the most effective path to resolution is to set aside judgment and emotion and work through the factual sequence of events. This means identifying who made which decision, whether there was genuine miscommunication, and what the realistic options for resolution are. Emotion clouds accountability and makes resolution harder.
teachings.quote: I want to break this down... we're gonna leave judgment and emotion over here right because I want to work through it and then come up with a reasonable solution.

teachings.concept: Own Your Mistakes Directly and Create Resolution Options
teachings.explanation: When a builder or PM made a decision without client sign-off and it turned out wrong, the right move is to acknowledge it plainly, explain how it happened, and present the client with resolution options. Transparency and honesty in these moments often repair trust rather than damage it further.
teachings.quote: A lot of times in construction we make decisions based on time frames or availability and expertise. The decision I made was to paint this semi-gloss because of x y and z... I may have made the wrong decision and how would you like me to handle this.

teachings.concept: Two-Year Positioning as the Filter for Saying No
teachings.explanation: Every decision a builder makes today positions their company one to two years from now. Using this long-view filter makes it much easier to decline work that doesn't align with the business's target market, niche, or values. Rather than saying no from scarcity, you say no because you are on a mission.
teachings.quote: Every decision and choice we make positions us a year or two from now 100 of the time... whatever is in your world the way I'm going to answer this particular question is in alignment with how I'm setting myself up and my company up two years from now.

teachings.concept: Builders Must Lead the Design Decision Process
teachings.explanation: Great builders do not wait for designers or architects to deliver decisions on their own timeline. Builders must proactively communicate what decisions are needed and when, using two-week and four-week look-ahead schedules and recurring outstanding items lists in weekly reports. The responsibility for keeping the project moving belongs to the builder, not the design team.
teachings.quote: I actually don't expect anything I'm going to take the lead and tell them what we need and when we need it and the way that we figure that part out is through the schedule.

teachings.concept: Explain the Why to Designers So They Understand Urgency
teachings.explanation: Designers and architects are not builders and do not naturally understand the downstream ripple effects of delayed decisions on scheduling, subcontractors, and client experience. Rather than expecting them to intuit urgency, builders should explicitly explain what is happening on the ground because of a late decision. This reframes the conversation from confrontation to collaboration.
teachings.quote: Builders are built to move projects forward crazy efficiently. Designers are built because they have extraordinary vision and creativity and they let it and things have to bubble... two different ball games.

teachings.concept: Team Alignment Before Client-Facing Communication on Modifications
teachings.explanation: Before any modification or change order is communicated to a client, every person on the internal team must fully understand and agree on what is being presented. A project manager who communicates a change order they do not fully understand, or who assumes fee waivers extend beyond what was agreed, creates confusion that appears dishonest even when it is not.
teachings.quote: Don't ever talk to a client unless you really understand what you're saying you really have to understand and justify.

teachings.concept: Growing Carefully Means Staying Aligned with Your Culture and Capacity
teachings.explanation: Sustainable growth in a construction business is not just about revenue targets but about maintaining the quality of care for clients and team. Scaling should only happen when there is genuine team capacity to still deliver as if building for a best friend. Financial mapping, bonus structures, and staffing decisions should all be made in concert.
teachings.quote: I love the idea of growing carefully because the word care is in there and that's a really important word because it explains exactly what your culture is.

actionable_advice: Before doing a blue tape walkthrough with a homeowner, walk the space yourself first, establish your own standard of approval, and declare it before handing over the tape.
Get crystal clear on the quality difference between contractor tiers so you can defend work within the lower tier's competency and identify the rare cases that genuinely fall outside it.
When a dispute arises, control the project timeline: give the client a specific completion window, schedule a meeting for after that window, and ask them not to evaluate incomplete work.
When you or your PM made a decision without client approval that turned out wrong, own it plainly, explain the reasoning, and ask the client how they would like it resolved.

Have conversations about significant misunderstandings in person or by phone, not over email or text, because written-only communication in disputes creates legal exposure and prevents genuine resolution.
Use two-week and four-week look-ahead schedules plus three-to-six month projections to proactively communicate needed design decisions to designers and architects before they become urgent.
Put outstanding design decisions on the weekly client and designer report and leave them on the list until they are resolved so the need is visible and persistent.
When a designer or architect is causing a bottleneck, go directly to the source of the blockage with them present rather than just following up with the designer alone.
Explain to designers the specific downstream effects of late decisions on scheduling, subcontractor availability, and client experience so they understand the urgency in builder terms.
Before issuing a change order to a client, make sure every team member involved understands and agrees on what is being presented, including what fees are or are not being waived.
Define your business niche and target market clearly and use your two-year positioning goal as a filter for every new opportunity so saying no becomes a strategic act rather than a personal one.
Map out revenue targets annually including number of projects, project values, margins, payroll, and bonus structures so growth decisions are grounded in financial reality.
Laminate paint schedules for each room and post them on site so designers, homeowners, and subcontractors can all see and verify the specs during walkthroughs.
client_challenges: A client chose a lower-cost painter to save approximately $30,000, was warned about quality differences including high-gloss work, and is now unhappy with the results and demanding the builder pay to repaint the entire house.

A client broke trust over a $1,000 budget overrun after having approved tens of thousands in personal budget increases, then disputed a paint finish on a final invoice claiming it was never approved, and began contacting vendors directly to pay them independently and bypass the builder.
A project manager misunderstood that a fee waiver on one set of rough-in modifications also applied to future modifications, creating confusion and apparent dishonesty with the client.

A client is upset because a spray foam roof decision was made too late to allow her preferred shingle color, and the designer may not even be aware of the client's frustration or the project manager's scramble to find alternatives.

A designer team is consistently late on decisions, causing the builder's project manager to drop core responsibilities like scheduling and quality control in order to chase down information and run sample errands.

A participant transitioning from tech project management into construction is being approached by local builders who want to hire her as a PM rather than supporting her goal of running her own GC company.

Participants across the call described difficulty getting designers and architects to deliver decisions on the builder's timeline because the design team does not understand the construction scheduling implications of their delays.
notable_quotes: On problems as opportunity: 'I was talking to my roofer today and he goes... God is my greatest salesperson. He probably has 50 phone calls today from people who need to re-roof their house. And I was like, how do I do that as a builder?'
On resolving disputes: 'Leave judgment and emotion over here because I want to work through it and then come up with a reasonable solution.'

On saying no to work: 'Every decision and choice we make positions us a year or two from now 100 of the time... the way I'm going to answer this particular question is in alignment with how I'm setting myself up and my company up two years from now.'
On growing carefully: 'I love the idea of growing carefully because the word care is in there and that's a really important word because it explains exactly what your culture is.'

On builders versus designers: 'Builders are built to move projects forward crazy efficiently. Designers are built because they have extraordinary vision and creativity and things have to bubble... two different ball games.'
On holding to a dream: 'You gotta hold to the dream. Unless your partner is going to divorce you, nothing else should stop you from going to it.'
